one_line_takeaway: VulnSage is a 593-vuln / 491-CVE C/C++ benchmark plus a two-stage noise-cleaning and multi-granularity pipeline showing that Think-and-Verify style structured reasoning nearly doubles zero-shot LLM vulnerability-detection accuracy (36.7% to 57.94%) and cuts ambiguous answers from 20.3% to 9.1%, with code-specialized models best at detection and reasoning models best at patch verification.
key_finding: Structured reasoning with explicit self-verification (Think & Verify) substantially improves LLM SVD: average vulnerability-detection accuracy rises from 36.70% (Baseline) to 57.94% (Think & Verify), and patch-verification accuracy from 33.98% to 57.70%; ambiguous 'not sure' responses fall from 20.3% (CoT) to 9.1% (Think & Verify). Code-specialized models lead detection (Codellama 7B best average vuln accuracy 63.70%) while reasoning models lead patch verification (Deepseek R1 32B 73.36% under Think & Verify), so no single model or prompt dominates all CWE types. A post-training-cutoff (2025) subset shows Think & Verify actually scoring HIGHER on unseen vulns (67.5%) than on the potentially-contaminated 2002-2019 set (57.94%), which the authors read as evidence of genuine reasoning over memorization. Granularity has a divergent effect: added cross-function/cross-file context tends to help vulnerability detection but hurts patch verification (e.g., Deepseek-v2 16B baseline patch accuracy drops from 7.41% at G1 to 1.86% at G3).
core_contribution: The paper introduces VulnSage, an evaluation framework AND a curated dataset for benchmarking pre-trained LLMs on zero-shot software vulnerability detection (SVD) in real-world C/C++ system software. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a dataset of 593 vulnerabilities spanning 52 CWE categories and 491 unique CVEs (years 2002-2019), curated from Linux Kernel, Mozilla, Gecko-dev, and Xen via CVEDetails metadata; (2) a two-stage noise-mitigation pipeline combining heuristic pre-filtering with an LLM-based noise-quantification reasoner (Deepseek R1 7B assigns a 0-100% non-security-noise score to each commit); (3) multi-granular code representations that preserve inter-function and cross-file relationships (granularity levels G1/G2/G3) rather than treating functions in isolation; and (4) four zero-shot prompting strategies of increasing reasoning depth (Baseline, Chain-of-Thought, Think, Think & Verify) applied across two tasks (vulnerability detection and patch verification). This is an empirical benchmarking/evaluation contribution, not a new detection model or an attack technique. NOTE: the prior-work critiques of single-granularity datasets, noise from refactoring/doc commits, and data-leakage in benchmarks are framing from Section 2 Related Work; VulnSage's claimed novelty is the combination of multi-granularity + automated noise quantification + post-training-cutoff leakage testing.
threat_model: Defensive/evaluative, not adversarial. The security context is exploitable weaknesses in widely deployed C/C++ system software (memory-safety bugs such as CWE-119 buffer overflow and CWE-416 use-after-free, access-control issues such as CWE-264 and CWE-284, race conditions CWE-362, NULL-pointer dereference CWE-476). The paper does not propose an attacker model or an exploit; it measures how well LLMs can flag such vulnerabilities and verify patches. The nearest thing to an adversarial concern the paper itself raises is a methodological one: training-data leakage / memorization, which it treats as a validity threat and probes by evaluating on a post-training-cutoff (2025) subset.
limitations: By the authors' own Threats to Validity: (1) residual dataset noise is unavoidable and the LLM-based noise scorer (Deepseek R1 7B) itself introduces model bias and may misclassify non-security changes; only 2% of the data was manually validated. (2) The leakage-free evaluation rests on just ten post-cutoff samples, so the anti-memorization conclusion is weakly powered. (3) Zero-shot only, no fine-tuning; and the true scope of each model's training corpus is unknown, leaving room for indirect leakage. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: accuracy is the sole metric (no precision/recall/F1 despite a class-imbalanced YES/NO task, and note the Baseline column contains extreme values like 93.93% and 0.34% that suggest degenerate all-yes/all-no answering rather than real detection skill); dataset is C/C++ system software 2002-2019 only; the 'twelve LLMs / 52 CWE' vs ten-models tabulation is internally inconsistent.

datasets_used: VulnSage (this paper's own dataset): 593 vulnerabilities, 491 unique CVEs, 52 unique CWEs, C/C++, years 2002-2019; sources Linux Kernel, Mozilla, Gecko-dev, Xen; avg 2.71 files changed, avg 18.42 functions changed, avg 296.79 lines in vulnerable code / 304.92 in patched
Granularity split within VulnSage: G1 single function 27 samples, G2 multiple functions single file 244, G3 multiple files & functions 322
Leakage-free subset: 10 vulnerabilities discovered in 2025 (after all evaluated models' training cutoffs)
quantitative_results: Vulnerability detection accuracy (overall avg): Baseline 36.70% -> Think & Verify 57.94% (+21.24 pts)
Patch verification accuracy (overall avg): Baseline 33.98% -> Think & Verify 57.70% (+23.72 pts)
Ambiguous 'not sure' responses: CoT 20.3% -> Think & Verify 9.1% (avg counts 120.3 -> 50.6 for vuln detection)
Best avg vuln-detection model: Codellama 7B 63.70%; best avg patch-verification model: Qwen2.5-coder 32B 65.14%
Best single scores: Codellama 34B 72.26% vuln detection (Think & Verify); Deepseek R1 32B 73.36% patch verification (Think & Verify)
Leakage-free 2025 subset: Think & Verify 67.5% vuln accuracy vs 57.94% on 2002-2019 set; overall Think and Think & Verify both 67.5% vuln
Noise robustness at highest noise level: accuracy decline Think & Verify 2.2%, Think 2.9%, Baseline 6.4%
Granularity effect on patch verification (Deepseek-v2 16B, baseline): 7.41% (G1) -> 1.86% (G3); CodeLlama 34B Think vuln detection 62.96% (G1) -> 74.80% (G2) -> 72.36% (G3)
Per-CWE examples (Think & Verify): Deepseek R1 7B 69.2% on CWE-362 race conditions, 65.1% on CWE-119 buffer overflow; Qwen2.5-coder 32B 70.3% on CWE-119
Most frequent CWEs in dataset: CWE-119 (58), CWE-264 (48), CWE-416 (41), CWE-284 (30); ~39% of initial data removed by heuristic pre-filtering
baselines_compared: Prompt-strategy baselines: Baseline (binary YES/NO) vs CoT vs Think vs Think & Verify

Model-family comparison: general-purpose (Deepseek-v2 16B, Llama3.1 8B, Gemma2 9B) vs code-specialized (Deepseek-coder-v2 16B, Qwen2.5-coder 7B, Qwen2.5-coder 32B, Codellama 7B, Codellama 34B) vs reasoning-focused (Deepseek R1 7B, Deepseek R1 32B)
Potentially-contaminated 2002-2019 dataset vs leakage-free 2025 subset (memorization control)
No prior detection tool or published SOTA method is used as a numeric baseline; comparison is internal across prompts, models, granularities, and noise levels
